q: Which DOCTYPE declaration is correct for an HTML5 document?
explanation: HTML5 uses the simplified DOCTYPE declaration '<!DOCTYPE html>'. Unlike HTML 4.01 or XHTML which required long DOCTYPE strings referencing DTDs, HTML5 simplified this to just '<!DOCTYPE html>' â€” case-insensitive and requires no URL reference.

q: Which character encoding declaration is recommended for HTML5 documents?
explanation: HTML5 introduced the shorthand '<meta charset="UTF-8">' for declaring character encoding. UTF-8 is the recommended encoding as it supports all Unicode characters. This tag must appear within the first 1024 bytes of the document.

q: Which of the following is a semantic HTML5 tag used to define the main navigation links of a page?
explanation: The <nav> element is a semantic HTML5 tag that defines a set of navigation links. It helps screen readers and search engines identify the primary navigation section of a webpage, improving accessibility and SEO.

q: Which HTML5 semantic element is used to represent self-contained content that could be independently distributed?
explanation: The <article> element represents self-contained content that can be independently distributed or reused â€” such as a blog post, news article, or forum post. Unlike <section> which groups thematically related content, <article> implies independence.

q: What is the correct HTML5 element for content that is tangentially related to the main content (like a sidebar)?
explanation: The <aside> element represents content that is tangentially related to the surrounding content â€” typically rendered as a sidebar. Examples include pull quotes, related links, advertising, or author bios that supplement the main content.

q: Which HTML5 element defines the footer of a document or section?
explanation: The <footer> element defines the footer for a document or section. It typically contains authorship information, copyright notices, contact information, or links to related documents. A page can have multiple <footer> elements.

q: Which HTML5 element is used to group introductory content or navigational aids at the top of a page?
explanation: The <header> element represents introductory content or a set of navigational links. It typically contains a heading, logo, search form, or author name. Unlike <head> (which contains metadata), <header> is visible page content.

q: Which HTML5 element is used to define thematically grouped content with a heading?
explanation: The <section> element defines a thematic grouping of content, typically with a heading. It differs from <article> in that its content may not stand alone independently. Use <section> to divide content into meaningful chapters or topics.

q: Which of the following is a void element in HTML?
explanation:
A void element is one that cannot have any child content and has no closing tag.

explanation:
Block-level elements start on a new line and take up the full width available.

q: Which of the following is an inline element in HTML?
explanation:
Inline elements do not start on a new line and only take up as much width as necessary. is an inline element. Other inline elements include , , , , . Block elements like

q: Which HTML attribute specifies an alternative text for an image if it cannot be displayed?
explanation: The 'alt' attribute provides alternative text for an image when it cannot be displayed. It is also read by screen readers for accessibility. The 'alt' attribute is required for elements according to HTML standards and WCAG accessibility guidelines.

q: What is the correct HTML for creating a hyperlink?
explanation: The <a> (anchor) element with the 'href' attribute creates a hyperlink. 'href' stands for Hypertext REFerence and specifies the URL destination. The text between the tags becomes the clickable link text.

q: Which attribute opens a hyperlink in a new browser tab?
explanation: The attribute target="_blank" opens the linked document in a new browser tab or window. Other target values include '_self' (same frame, default), '_parent' (parent frame), and '_top' (full window). For security, use rel="noopener noreferrer" with target="_blank".

q: Which HTML input type is used to create a checkbox?
explanation: The input type 'checkbox' creates a checkbox that allows users to select one or more options. When selected, it submits the value defined by its 'value' attribute. The 'checked' attribute can pre-select the checkbox by default.

q: Which HTML5 input type is used for entering an email address with built-in validation?
explanation: HTML5 introduced <input type="email"> which validates that the entered value matches email format (contains @ symbol). It provides native browser validation, appropriate keyboard on mobile devices, and styling hooks for invalid inputs via CSS.

q: Which HTML5 input type displays a slider control?
explanation: The <input type="range"> creates a slider control for selecting a numeric value within a specified range. Attributes 'min', 'max', and 'step' control the range and increment. It is ideal for settings like volume or brightness controls.

q: Which attribute makes an input field mandatory before form submission?
explanation: The 'required' attribute is a boolean attribute that prevents form submission if the field is empty. It provides built-in HTML5 validation. When a required field is empty and the user submits, the browser shows a validation error without JavaScript.

q: Which HTML element is used to group related form controls with a visible border?
explanation: The <fieldset> element groups related form controls with a border. It is used with the <legend> element which provides a caption for the group. This improves form accessibility and visual organization, especially for complex forms with multiple sections.

q: Which HTML5 attribute provides a short hint describing the expected input in a form field?
explanation: The 'placeholder' attribute displays a short hint (ghost text) inside an input field that describes the expected value. It disappears when the user starts typing. Note that placeholder text should not replace labels as it has accessibility limitations.

q: In an HTML table, which element defines a header cell?
explanation: <th> defines a header cell in an HTML table. By default, header cells are bold and centered. The 'scope' attribute (scope="col" or scope="row") improves accessibility by associating headers with data cells for screen readers.

q: Which HTML attribute allows a table cell to span multiple columns?
explanation: The 'colspan' attribute on a <td> or <th> element specifies how many columns that cell should span. Similarly, 'rowspan' spans multiple rows. Example: <td colspan="3"> merges three consecutive column cells into one.

q: Which HTML element defines the body of an HTML table (excluding header and footer rows)?
explanation: The <tbody> element groups the body rows of an HTML table, distinct from <thead> (header rows) and <tfoot> (footer rows). This semantic grouping allows browsers to render large tables more efficiently and enables independent scrolling of the table body.

q: Which HTML element creates an ordered (numbered) list?
explanation: The <ol> (ordered list) element creates a numbered list where order matters. Each item uses <li>. In contrast, <ul> (unordered list) uses bullet points. The 'type' attribute can change numbering style (1, A, a, I, i) and 'start' sets the starting number.

q: Which HTML element is used for a definition list (term-definition pairs)?
explanation: The <dl> (description/definition list) element is used for term-definition pairs. It contains <dt> (description term) and <dd> (description details) elements. It's useful for glossaries, metadata displays, and key-value pairs.

q: Which meta tag is used to set the viewport for responsive web design?
explanation: The viewport meta tag '<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">' is essential for responsive design. It instructs the browser to set the viewport width to the device width and sets the initial zoom level to 1. Without it, mobile browsers render pages at desktop width.

q: Which meta tag is used to specify a description of the webpage for search engines?
explanation: The <meta name="description"> tag provides a brief description of the webpage's content for search engines. Search engines often display this text as the snippet under the page title in search results. It's important for SEO (Search Engine Optimization).

q: Which HTML5 element is used to embed audio content in a webpage?
explanation: The HTML5 <audio> element embeds audio content. It supports attributes like 'controls' (show play/pause UI), 'autoplay', 'loop', and 'muted'. Multiple <source> elements can be nested to provide audio in different formats (MP3, OGG, WAV) for cross-browser compatibility.

q: Which HTML5 element is used to embed video content in a webpage?
explanation: The HTML5 <video> element embeds video content natively without plugins. Key attributes include 'controls', 'autoplay', 'loop', 'muted', 'width', and 'height'. The 'poster' attribute specifies an image to show before the video plays. Supports MP4, WebM, and Ogg formats.

q: Which HTML5 element provides a 2D drawing surface controlled via JavaScript?
explanation: The HTML5 <canvas> element provides a resolution-dependent bitmap canvas for drawing graphics via JavaScript. It uses the Canvas 2D API (getContext('2d')) or WebGL for 3D graphics. Unlike SVG, canvas is raster-based and not part of the DOM tree once drawn.

q: What is the key difference between HTML5 Canvas and SVG?
options: Canvas is vector-based; SVG is raster-based
Canvas is raster-based (pixel); SVG is vector-based (XML)
Canvas supports DOM manipulation; SVG does not
SVG requires JavaScript; Canvas does not
explanation: Canvas is raster/bitmap-based â€” it draws pixels and loses quality when scaled. SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) is vector-based using XML, so it scales without quality loss. SVG elements are part of the DOM and can be styled with CSS. Canvas is better for complex animations; SVG for icons and diagrams.

q: Which HTML5 element is used to embed scalable vector graphics defined in XML?
explanation: The <svg> element embeds Scalable Vector Graphics inline in HTML. SVG graphics are defined using XML markup and are part of the DOM, making them accessible and styleable with CSS. They scale perfectly at any resolution, making them ideal for logos, icons, and charts.

q: Which HTML form method sends data appended to the URL in the query string?
explanation: The GET method appends form data to the URL as a query string (e.g., ?name=value&age=25). It's suitable for non-sensitive data, bookmarkable URLs, and search queries. POST sends data in the request body, making it suitable for sensitive data, file uploads, and large data.

q: Which HTML attribute specifies the URL to which form data is sent when submitted?
explanation: The 'action' attribute of the <form> element specifies the URL (server-side script) to which form data is submitted. If omitted, the form submits to the current page's URL. The 'method' attribute specifies the HTTP method (GET or POST).

q: Which HTML5 input type automatically validates that the entry is a URL?
explanation: HTML5's <input type="url"> validates that the entered value is a properly formatted URL (must begin with http:// or https://). It provides native validation, appropriate on-screen keyboard on mobile devices, and integrates with the browser's form validation system.

q: Which HTML attribute is used to define the maximum number of characters allowed in an input field?
explanation: The 'maxlength' attribute specifies the maximum number of characters that can be entered in an <input> or <textarea> element. For numeric inputs, 'max' restricts the maximum value. The 'size' attribute controls the visual width of the input field.

q: Which HTML element is used to create a drop-down selection list?
explanation: The <select> element creates a drop-down list. It contains <option> elements for each choice. The 'multiple' attribute allows selecting multiple options. The 'size' attribute controls how many options are visible. The selected option's 'value' attribute is submitted with the form.

q: Which HTML5 element provides an autocomplete list of predefined options for an input?
explanation: The HTML5 <datalist> element provides a list of predefined autocomplete options for an <input> element. Link them using the 'list' attribute on the input and the 'id' on datalist. Unlike <select>, users can still type custom values not in the list.

q: What does the 'enctype' attribute in an HTML form specify?
options: The character encoding of the form
How form data is encoded when submitted
The encryption algorithm for secure forms
The content type of the response
explanation: The 'enctype' attribute specifies how form data is encoded when submitted. Default is 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'. Use 'multipart/form-data' for forms with file uploads (<input type="file">). 'text/plain' sends data without encoding (rarely used).

q: Which HTML5 input type is used for selecting a date (year, month, day)?
explanation: HTML5's <input type="date"> displays a date picker widget in supporting browsers for selecting year, month, and day. The value format is YYYY-MM-DD. Related types include 'time' (HH:MM), 'datetime-local' (date and time), 'month', and 'week'.

q: Which HTML attribute disables an input field so it cannot be modified or submitted?
explanation: The 'disabled' attribute prevents an input from being modified, focused, or submitted with the form. A disabled field's value is NOT submitted. The 'readonly' attribute also prevents modification but the field is still focusable and its value IS submitted.

q: In HTML, the 'name' attribute of an input element is used for what purpose?
options: It labels the field for the user to see
It identifies the field's value when the form is submitted
It specifies the CSS class of the field
It sets the default value of the field
explanation: The 'name' attribute of form elements identifies the data when submitted to the server. Form data is sent as name=value pairs. Without a 'name' attribute, the input's value is not submitted. It is different from 'id', which is used for JavaScript/CSS targeting.

q: Which HTML element is used for multi-line text input?
explanation: The <textarea> element creates a multi-line text input field. Its size is controlled by 'rows' and 'cols' attributes or CSS. Unlike <input type="text">, it allows users to enter multiple lines of text. The default value is placed between the opening and closing tags.

q: What is the purpose of the HTML <label> element?
options: To associate a text description with a form control for accessibility
To add a tooltip to an input
explanation: The <label> element associates a text description with a form control. Using the 'for' attribute matching the input's 'id', clicking the label focuses/activates the input. This improves accessibility for screen readers and increases the clickable area, especially useful for checkboxes and radio buttons.

q: Which HTML5 attribute can validate an input field value against a regular expression pattern?
explanation: The HTML5 'pattern' attribute specifies a regular expression that the input's value must match for the form to be valid. For example, pattern="[A-Za-z]{3}" requires exactly 3 letters. It works with text, tel, email, url, password, and search input types.

q: Which HTML element is used to define a table row?
explanation: The <tr> (table row) element defines a row in an HTML table. Each row contains one or more <td> (table data cell) or <th> (table header cell) elements. Rows are grouped using <thead>, <tbody>, and <tfoot> for semantic structure.

q: Which HTML tag is used to define the title of a webpage shown in the browser's title bar and tabs?
explanation: The <title> element defines the document's title shown in the browser tab, title bar, and search engine results. It must be inside the <head> element. It is important for SEO and bookmarking. It differs from heading tags (<h1>-<h6>) which are visible page content.

q: Which HTML element is used to link an external CSS stylesheet?
explanation: The <link> element inside <head> links external resources, primarily CSS stylesheets. Example: <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">. The 'rel' attribute specifies the relationship. It is a void element. The <style> element is for internal CSS, not external files.

q: In HTML5, which attribute on the <script> tag allows the script to execute after the page has parsed without blocking rendering?
explanation: The 'defer' attribute makes the browser download the script without blocking HTML parsing and executes it only after the document is fully parsed. 'async' also downloads without blocking but executes immediately when downloaded, potentially before parsing finishes. 'defer' preserves script execution order; 'async' does not.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the HTML5 document structure order?
explanation: The correct HTML5 document structure is: <!DOCTYPE html> declaration first, then <html> root element containing <head> (metadata section) followed by <body> (visible content section). The <head> must come before <body>. The DOCTYPE declaration must be the very first line.

q: Which HTML5 input type is used for entering a telephone number?
explanation: HTML5's <input type="tel"> is used for telephone number input. Unlike type="number", it doesn't restrict non-numeric characters (since phone formats vary globally) but triggers a numeric keypad on mobile devices. The 'pattern' attribute is commonly used with it for validation.

q: Which CSS selector targets an element with the class 'highlight'?
explanation: Class selectors use a dot (.) prefix. '.highlight' selects all elements with class='highlight', while '#highlight' would be an ID selector.

q: What is the correct CSS syntax to select an element with id='header'?
explanation: ID selectors use the hash (#) prefix. '#header' selects the unique element with id='header'. IDs should be unique within a page.

q:
Which CSS selector selects all

elements inside a

?
explanation:
'div p' is a descendant selector that selects all

elements anywhere inside a

. 'div > p' selects only direct children.

q: Which attribute selector selects elements whose 'href' attribute value starts with 'https'?
explanation: The [attr^=value] selector matches elements where the attribute value begins with the specified value. ^ means 'starts with', $ means 'ends with', * means 'contains'.

q: Which pseudo-class selector applies styles to a link that has been visited?
explanation: :visited pseudo-class applies styles to links the user has already visited. The order LVHA (:link, :visited, :hover, :active) is recommended to avoid specificity issues.

q: Which pseudo-element adds content before an element's content?
explanation: ::before pseudo-element inserts content before the element's actual content. It requires the 'content' property to be set. ::after inserts content after.

q: In the CSS box model, what is the correct order from outermost to innermost?
explanation: The CSS box model layers from outside to inside are: Margin (outermost, transparent space outside border) > Border > Padding > Content (innermost).

q: Which CSS property sets the space between the border and the content of an element?
explanation: Padding is the space between an element's content and its border. Margin is the space outside the border between elements.

q: When using 'box-sizing: border-box', what does the declared width include?
explanation: With 'box-sizing: border-box', the width and height properties include content, padding, and border â€” but NOT margin. This makes layout sizing more intuitive.

q: What is margin collapsing in CSS?
options: Top and bottom margins of adjacent block elements combine into a single margin
Horizontal margins of inline elements collapse to zero
explanation: Margin collapsing occurs when top and bottom margins of adjacent block-level elements combine into a single margin equal to the larger of the two. It does NOT happen with horizontal margins.

q: Which CSS property is used to make an element a flex container?
explanation: 'display: flex' makes an element a flex container, enabling Flexbox layout for its direct children (flex items). 'display: inline-flex' creates an inline flex container.

q: Which flexbox property aligns flex items along the main axis?
explanation: 'justify-content' aligns flex items along the main axis (horizontally by default). 'align-items' aligns items along the cross axis. Common values: flex-start, flex-end, center, space-between, space-around.

q: What does 'flex: 1' mean in CSS Flexbox?
explanation: 'flex: 1' is shorthand for flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 0%. It allows the item to grow and shrink equally, distributing available space evenly among items with flex: 1.

q: Which flexbox property changes the direction of the main axis to column?
explanation: 'flex-direction: column' sets the main axis vertically (top to bottom). Default is 'row' (left to right). 'column-reverse' reverses the column direction.

q: Which CSS Grid property defines the number and size of columns in a grid?
explanation: 'grid-template-columns' defines the column structure of a grid container. Example: 'grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 1fr' creates three columns â€” one fixed and two equal flexible columns.

q: What does the CSS Grid value 'fr' represent?
explanation: 'fr' (fractional unit) represents a fraction of the available space in the grid container. '1fr 2fr' means the second column gets twice the space of the first.

q: Which CSS Grid function creates repeated column/row patterns?
explanation: The repeat() function creates repeated track patterns. Example: 'grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr)' creates 3 equal columns. Can be combined with auto-fill/auto-fit.

q: Which CSS position value removes an element from the normal document flow and positions it relative to its nearest positioned ancestor?
explanation: 'position: absolute' removes the element from the document flow and positions it relative to the nearest ancestor with position other than static. If none exists, it's positioned relative to the initial containing block.

q: Which CSS position value keeps an element fixed relative to the viewport during scrolling?
explanation: 'position: fixed' positions the element relative to the viewport and it stays in place during scrolling. Unlike absolute, it's not affected by scrolling the page.

q: What is the behavior of 'position: sticky'?
options: Always fixed at top of page
Relative until scroll threshold, then fixed
explanation: 'position: sticky' acts as relative positioning until the element reaches a specified scroll threshold (using top/bottom), then behaves like fixed. Useful for sticky headers/navigation.

q: What is the default value of the CSS 'position' property?
explanation: The default value of 'position' is 'static'. Statically positioned elements follow the normal document flow and top/left/bottom/right/z-index properties have no effect on them.

q: Calculate the specificity of the selector: '#nav .menu li:hover'
explanation: Specificity is calculated as (inline, ID, class/pseudo-class/attribute, element/pseudo-element). #nav = 0,1,0,0; .menu = 0,0,1,0; li = 0,0,0,1; :hover = 0,0,1,0. Total: 0,1,2,1.

q: Which has the highest CSS specificity?
explanation: Specificity order (lowest to highest): Universal (*) < Element < Class/Pseudo-class/Attribute < ID < Inline styles < !important. ID selectors have higher specificity than class selectors.

q: Which CSS property is NOT inherited by default?
explanation: Border is NOT inherited. Inherited properties include: color, font-size, font-family, font-weight, text-align, visibility, cursor. Box model properties (margin, padding, border) are NOT inherited.

q: Which CSS value explicitly forces a property to inherit its value from the parent element?
explanation: The 'inherit' keyword forces a property to take the computed value of its parent element, even for non-inherited properties. 'initial' resets to browser default, 'unset' resets to either inherited or initial value.

q: Which media query applies styles for screens with a maximum width of 768px?
explanation: '@media screen and (max-width: 768px)' applies styles on screen devices with width â‰¤ 768px. 'screen' specifies the media type. 'max-width' is a media feature for mobile-first or responsive breakpoints.

q: What is the 'mobile-first' approach in responsive design with media queries?
options: Write desktop styles first, then override for mobile using max-width
Write mobile styles first, then override for larger screens using min-width
Use separate CSS files for mobile and desktop
explanation: Mobile-first means writing CSS for small screens first, then using min-width media queries to progressively enhance for larger screens. This is more performant as mobile devices don't load unused desktop styles.

q: Which CSS property creates a smooth transition between property values?
explanation: 'transition' enables smooth changes between CSS property values over a specified duration. Syntax: 'transition: property duration timing-function delay'. Example: 'transition: background-color 0.3s ease'.

q: What is the correct syntax for a CSS transition that animates 'opacity' over 0.5 seconds?
explanation: CSS transition shorthand syntax: 'transition: property duration timing-function delay'. 'transition: opacity 0.5s' is correct. The property name comes first, followed by duration.

q: Which CSS at-rule defines keyframes for an animation?
explanation: '@keyframes' defines the animation sequence by specifying styles at various points. Combined with 'animation-name', 'animation-duration', etc. Example: '@keyframes slidein { from { ... } to { ... } }'.

q: Which CSS transform function rotates an element 45 degrees clockwise?
explanation: 'transform: rotate(45deg)' rotates an element 45 degrees clockwise. Negative values rotate counter-clockwise. Other transform functions: translate(), scale(), skew(), matrix().

q: Which CSS property controls the stacking order of positioned elements?
explanation: 'z-index' controls the stacking order along the z-axis. Higher values appear on top. It only works on positioned elements (position other than static) and flex/grid items. Default is 'auto'.

q: An element with z-index: 10 and another with z-index: 5 â€” which appears on top?
options: The one declared later in CSS
z-index has no effect without position
explanation: Higher z-index values place elements closer to the viewer (on top). z-index: 10 appears above z-index: 5. Note: z-index only works on positioned elements (not position: static).

q: Which CSS property is used to float an element to the left?
explanation: 'float: left' takes an element out of normal flow and places it to the left, allowing text and inline elements to wrap around it. Values: left, right, none, inline-start, inline-end.

q: Which CSS property is used to prevent an element from wrapping around floated elements?
explanation: 'clear: both' prevents an element from sitting next to floated elements on either side. Values: left (clears left floats), right (clears right floats), both (clears all floats), none.

q: What is the 'clearfix' hack used for in CSS?
options: To fix browser compatibility for colors
To make a container expand to contain its floated children
explanation: A floated element's parent collapses its height. The clearfix hack (using ::after pseudo-element with content:'', display:block, clear:both) forces the parent to contain its floated children.

q: Which CSS3 feature allows creating smooth color transitions as backgrounds?
explanation: 'linear-gradient()' creates a linear gradient background. Syntax: 'background: linear-gradient(direction, color1, color2)'. 'radial-gradient()' creates circular/elliptical gradients. These are CSS3 features.

q: Which CSS property adds a shadow to text?
explanation: 'text-shadow' adds shadow to text. Syntax: 'text-shadow: h-offset v-offset blur-radius color'. 'box-shadow' adds shadows to elements (boxes). Both are CSS3 features.

q: Which CSS property rounds the corners of an element's border?
explanation: 'border-radius' rounds an element's corners. 'border-radius: 50%' creates a circle/oval. Individual corners: border-top-left-radius, border-top-right-radius, etc. This is a CSS3 property.

q: In Bootstrap, which class makes a column take up half the width of a 12-column grid on medium screens?
explanation: Bootstrap uses a 12-column grid. 'col-md-6' takes up 6 of 12 columns = 50% width on medium (md, â‰¥768px) and larger screens. 'col-md-4' = 33.33%, 'col-md-3' = 25%.

q: Which Bootstrap class creates a responsive container with a maximum width at each breakpoint?
explanation: 'container' creates a responsive fixed-width container (has max-width at each breakpoint). 'container-fluid' creates a full-width container that spans the entire viewport at all breakpoints.

q: What is the viewport meta tag used for in responsive design?
options: Controls the viewport's width and scaling on mobile devices
explanation: The viewport meta tag '<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">' tells mobile browsers to set the viewport width to the device's width and initial zoom to 1, enabling proper responsive behavior.

q: Which CSS pseudo-class selects the first child element of its parent?
explanation: ':first-child' selects an element that is the first child of its parent. ':first-of-type' selects the first element of its type. ':nth-child(1)' is equivalent to ':first-child' (CSS uses 1-based indexing).

q: Which CSS pseudo-class selects every odd-numbered element?
explanation: ':nth-child(odd)' or equivalently ':nth-child(2n+1)' selects odd-numbered children (1st, 3rd, 5th...). ':nth-child(even)' or ':nth-child(2n)' selects even-numbered children.

q: Which pseudo-element styles only the first line of a text block?
explanation: '::first-line' pseudo-element applies styles to the first line of a block-level element. '::first-letter' styles only the first letter. These are called pseudo-elements and use double colon (::) notation in CSS3.

q: What does 'display: none' do to an element compared to 'visibility: hidden'?
options: display:none hides and removes from flow; visibility:hidden hides but keeps space
display:none keeps space; visibility:hidden removes from flow
display:none makes transparent; visibility:hidden removes element
explanation: 'display: none' completely removes the element from the document flow (no space occupied). 'visibility: hidden' hides the element but preserves its space in the layout. opacity: 0 also preserves space and is visible to screen readers.

q: Which CSS property defines how an element handles content that overflows its box?
explanation: 'overflow' controls what happens when content exceeds an element's dimensions. Values: visible (default, content overflows), hidden (clips overflow), scroll (always shows scrollbars), auto (scrollbars when needed).

q: Which CSS Grid property allows an item to span multiple columns?
explanation: 'grid-column: span 2' makes a grid item span 2 columns. Can also use 'grid-column: 1 / 3' (start line 1 to end line 3). 'grid-row: span 2' does the same for rows.

q: Which CSS animation property controls the number of times an animation plays?
explanation: 'animation-iteration-count' specifies how many times an animation repeats. Values: a number (1, 2, etc.) or 'infinite' for endless looping. Default is 1.

q: Which CSS property is used to apply multiple transformations to an element?
explanation: Multiple CSS transform functions are applied by space-separating them in a single transform property: 'transform: rotate(45deg) scale(1.5) translateX(20px)'. Separate transform declarations overwrite each other.

q: In Bootstrap's grid system, what breakpoint prefix is used for extra-small screens (<576px)?
explanation: In Bootstrap 4/5, 'col-' (no infix) targets extra-small screens (<576px). Breakpoints: col- (xs, <576px), col-sm- (â‰¥576px), col-md- (â‰¥768px), col-lg- (â‰¥992px), col-xl- (â‰¥1200px), col-xxl- (â‰¥1400px).

q: Which CSS property specifies the speed curve of a CSS transition or animation?
explanation: 'animation-timing-function' (or 'transition-timing-function') controls the speed curve. Values: ease (default), linear, ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out, cubic-bezier(). Controls acceleration/deceleration patterns.

q: Which of the following is NOT a primitive data type in JavaScript?
explanation: In JavaScript, primitive types are: string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, and null. 'object' is a non-primitive (reference) type.

q: What is the output of: console.log(typeof null)?
explanation: typeof null returns 'object' â€” this is a well-known JavaScript bug that has been kept for backward compatibility since the language's early days.

q: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable that cannot be reassigned?
explanation: const declares a block-scoped binding that cannot be reassigned. Note: const objects can still have their properties mutated; only the binding itself is constant.

q: What is the scope of a variable declared with var inside a function?
explanation: var is function-scoped (or globally scoped if declared outside a function). Unlike let/const, var does not respect block boundaries such as if/for blocks.

q: What does JavaScript hoisting do to var declarations?
options: Moves the declaration and initialization to the top of the scope
Moves only the declaration to the top; initialization stays in place
Ignores the variable until it is encountered
Throws a ReferenceError before the declaration
explanation: Hoisting moves the declaration to the top of the function/global scope but NOT the initialization. Accessing a var before its assignment returns undefined, not an error.

q: What happens when you access a let variable before its declaration in the same block?
explanation: let and const variables exist in the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) from the start of the block until the declaration is evaluated. Accessing them in the TDZ throws a ReferenceError.

q: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: A function that has no return value
A function bundled with its lexical environment, retaining access to outer scope variables
A method defined inside a class
A function that executes immediately after definition
explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) lexical scope even after that outer function has returned. This is fundamental to data encapsulation in JavaScript.

q: Consider: function outer(){ let x=10; return function inner(){ return x; } } What does outer()() return?
explanation: inner() forms a closure over outer()'s scope and retains access to x=10 even after outer() has returned. So outer()() returns 10.

q: Which concept allows a function to access variables from its containing scope?
explanation: Lexical (static) scoping means a function's scope is determined by where it is written in the source code, not where it is called. Closures rely on lexical scoping.

q: What does Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) return?
explanation: Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) is the standard ES5+ way to retrieve the prototype (internal [[Prototype]]) of an object, equivalent to obj.__proto__ but preferred in production code.

q: In the prototype chain, where does the lookup stop if a property is not found?
explanation: The prototype chain ends at null. Object.prototype's prototype is null, so if a property is not found anywhere in the chain, the lookup terminates at null and returns undefined.

q: Which ES6 feature allows you to unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables?
explanation: Destructuring assignment lets you extract values from arrays (e.g., const [a,b]=arr) or objects (e.g., const {x,y}=obj) into named variables.

q: What does the spread operator (...) do when used with an array?
options: Collects remaining elements into an array
Expands an iterable into individual elements
Creates a deep copy of an object
explanation: The spread operator expands an iterable (array, string, etc.) into individual elements. E.g., Math.max(...[1,2,3]) passes 1,2,3 as separate arguments.

q: What is the difference between spread (...) and rest (...) syntax?
options: Spread expands; rest collects â€” context determines which applies
Rest expands an array; spread collects arguments
Spread works only on objects; rest works only on arrays
explanation: Same syntax, different contexts: spread expands an iterable into elements (function call/array literal); rest collects multiple function arguments into a single array (function parameter list).

q: Which of the following correctly defines a template literal?
explanation: Template literals use backticks (`) and support embedded expressions via ${expression}. They also support multi-line strings natively.

q: How does an arrow function differ from a regular function regarding the 'this' keyword?
options: Arrow functions create their own 'this' binding
Arrow functions inherit 'this' from the enclosing lexical context
Arrow functions always bind 'this' to the global object
Arrow functions bind 'this' to the DOM element
explanation: Arrow functions do NOT have their own 'this'. They capture 'this' from the surrounding lexical scope at the time of creation. This makes them ideal for callbacks inside methods.

q: Which ES6 syntax defines a class with a constructor in JavaScript?
explanation: ES6 introduced the class keyword with a constructor method. The class syntax is syntactic sugar over JavaScript's prototype-based inheritance.

q: What keyword is used in ES6 modules to export a binding from a module?
explanation: ES6 modules use 'export' to expose bindings. Named exports: export const x=1; Default exports: export default function(){}. Consumers use 'import' to load them.

q: Which statement correctly imports a default export from a module file 'utils.js'?
explanation: Default exports are imported without curly braces: import utils from './utils.js'. The local name (utils) can be anything. Named exports require curly braces: import { namedExport } from './utils.js'.

q: What does a Promise represent in JavaScript?
options: A synchronous value that resolves immediately
An eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation
A generator function that yields values
A callback registered on the event loop
explanation: A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion (resolve) or failure (reject) of an asynchronous operation and its resulting value. It can be in pending, fulfilled, or rejected state.

q: Which Promise method executes when a Promise is successfully resolved?
explanation: .then(onFulfilled, onRejected) handles a resolved Promise. The first argument handles success; the optional second handles rejection. .catch(fn) is shorthand for .then(undefined, fn).

q: What does Promise.all([p1, p2, p3]) do?
options: Resolves as soon as any one Promise resolves
Rejects as soon as any one Promise resolves
Resolves when ALL Promises resolve; rejects if ANY rejects
Resolves with the first settled Promise result
explanation: Promise.all() waits for all Promises to fulfill and resolves with an array of results. If any Promise rejects, the entire Promise.all() rejects with that reason immediately.

q: What is the purpose of the async keyword in a function declaration?
options: Makes the function execute in a separate thread
Makes the function always return a Promise
Pauses execution until a value is returned
explanation: An async function always returns a Promise. If it returns a non-Promise value, it is automatically wrapped in a resolved Promise. This enables use of await inside the function.

q: What does the await keyword do inside an async function?
options: Pauses execution of the async function until the Promise settles
Creates a new Promise from a callback
Converts the function to synchronous execution
explanation: await pauses execution of the async function until the awaited Promise resolves or rejects, then resumes with the resolved value (or throws on rejection). It does NOT block the main thread.

q: Which method is used to select a DOM element by its ID?
explanation: document.getElementById('id') returns the element with the specified ID. It returns null if no match is found. For CSS selector syntax, document.querySelector('#id') also works.

q: Which DOM method returns all elements matching a CSS selector?
explanation: document.querySelectorAll(selector) returns a static NodeList of all elements matching the given CSS selector. document.querySelector() returns only the first match.

q: How do you add a new child element to a parent DOM node?
explanation: parent.appendChild(child) appends the child node to the end of parent's list of children. Modern alternative: parent.append(child) also accepts strings.

q: Which property sets or gets the HTML content inside a DOM element?
explanation: innerHTML gets/sets the HTML markup inside an element (parsed as HTML). textContent gets/sets only the text content (no HTML parsing). Use innerHTML carefully to avoid XSS vulnerabilities.

q: How do you attach an event listener to a button element in JavaScript?
explanation: addEventListener(type, handler) is the standard way to attach event listeners. It allows multiple handlers for the same event and supports capture/bubble phase control via the third argument.

q: In event bubbling, how does an event propagate through the DOM?
options: From the root down to the target element
From the target element up to the root (document)
Only on the target element, no propagation
explanation: Bubbling: the event fires on the target element first, then propagates up through parent elements to the document root. Capturing is the reverse: document â†’ target.

q: Which method stops an event from propagating further up the DOM in the bubbling phase?
explanation: event.stopPropagation() prevents the event from traveling further up (bubbling) or down (capturing) the DOM tree. event.preventDefault() only prevents the default browser action.

q: How do you register an event listener in the capturing phase?
explanation: The third argument to addEventListener controls the phase: true = capturing phase (document â†’ target); false (default) = bubbling phase (target â†’ document).

q: What does AJAX stand for?
explanation: AJAX = Asynchronous JavaScript And XML. It enables web pages to exchange data with a server in the background without a full page reload, though modern AJAX commonly uses JSON instead of XML.

q: Which object is used to make AJAX requests in older/traditional JavaScript?
explanation: XMLHttpRequest (XHR) is the original browser API for AJAX. It is event-driven and callback-based. The modern Fetch API provides a cleaner Promise-based alternative.

q: Which XMLHttpRequest readyState value indicates the request is complete?
explanation: XHR readyState values: 0=UNSENT, 1=OPENED, 2=HEADERS_RECEIVED, 3=LOADING, 4=DONE. When readyState===4, the response is fully received and ready to use.

q: Which Fetch API method sends a GET request and returns a Promise?
explanation: fetch(url) initiates a GET request and returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. By default, the method is GET. Use the second options argument for POST, headers, body, etc.

q: Which Fetch API Response method parses the response body as JSON?
explanation: response.json() reads the response stream to completion and parses the body as JSON, returning a Promise that resolves to the resulting JavaScript object.

q: What does JSON.stringify() do?
options: Parses a JSON string into a JavaScript object
Converts a JavaScript value into a JSON string
Validates a JSON string for syntax errors
explanation: JSON.stringify(value) serializes a JavaScript value (object, array, primitive) into a JSON-formatted string. Use the replacer and space arguments for filtering and pretty-printing.

q: What does JSON.parse() do?
options: Converts a JavaScript object to a JSON string
Fetches JSON from a remote URL
Parses a JSON string and returns the corresponding JavaScript value
Validates the structure of a JSON object
explanation: JSON.parse(text) deserializes a JSON string into a JavaScript value. It throws a SyntaxError if the input is not valid JSON.

q: What is the output of: JSON.parse('{"a":1}').a?
explanation: JSON.parse('{"a":1}') produces the JavaScript object {a: 1}. Accessing .a returns the number 1.

q: Which of the following values is NOT valid in JSON?
explanation: JSON does not support 'undefined'. Valid JSON values are: strings, numbers, objects, arrays, true, false, and null. JSON.stringify() omits object properties with undefined values.

q: What does the 'extends' keyword do in an ES6 class definition?
options: Copies all methods from one class to another
Creates a subclass that inherits from a parent class
explanation: class Child extends Parent sets up prototype-based inheritance so Child instances inherit Parent's methods. The child must call super() in its constructor before accessing 'this'.

q: What is the purpose of the 'super' keyword in a subclass constructor?
options: Calls the parent class's static method
Calls the parent class's constructor to initialize inherited properties
Points to the sibling class in the hierarchy
explanation: super() in a subclass constructor calls the parent class constructor. It must be called before accessing 'this' in the child constructor, or a ReferenceError is thrown.

q: Which of the following correctly uses object destructuring with a default value?
explanation: In destructuring, default values are assigned with = inside the pattern: const { x = 10 } = obj. If obj.x is undefined, x takes the default value of 10.

q: What is the output of: console.log([...'abc'])?
explanation: The spread operator spreads an iterable character by character. A string is iterable, so ...'abc' produces the three elements 'a', 'b', 'c', giving ['a','b','c'].

q: Which ES6 feature allows computed property names in object literals?
explanation: ES6 allows computed property names in object literals with bracket notation: const key='name'; const obj = { [key]: 'Alice' }; // { name: 'Alice' }.

q: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API compared to XMLHttpRequest?
options: Fetch is synchronous; XHR is asynchronous
Fetch uses Promises and is more modern and readable than XHR's callback model
Fetch supports only GET requests; XHR supports all HTTP methods
Fetch automatically parses JSON; XHR does not
explanation: Fetch API uses Promises for cleaner async code, supports async/await, and has a more modern interface. XHR uses events/callbacks, which can lead to callback hell. Both support all HTTP methods.

q: What is event delegation in JavaScript?
options: Adding separate event listeners to each child element
Attaching one listener on a parent element to handle events from child elements via bubbling
Preventing an event from reaching child elements
Delegating event processing to a Web Worker
explanation: Event delegation uses event bubbling: a single listener on a parent handles events for many children. Check event.target to identify the actual source. More efficient than adding listeners to each child.

q: Which property of an event object gives the element that originally triggered the event?
explanation: event.target is the element that originally triggered the event. event.currentTarget is the element the listener is attached to (can differ during bubbling). Used in event delegation.

q: What is the output of: (function(){ var x=1; if(true){ var x=2; } console.log(x); })()?
explanation: var is function-scoped, not block-scoped. The second var x=2 inside the if block is the same variable as the outer var x=1. So console.log(x) prints 2.

q: Which method creates a new array with the results of calling a function on every element?
explanation: Array.map(fn) returns a new array where each element is the result of calling fn on the corresponding element of the original array. Unlike forEach, it returns a new array.

q: What is the output of: typeof undefined === typeof null?
explanation: typeof undefined is 'undefined'; typeof null is 'object'. These are different strings, so the strict equality check returns false.

q: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve a resource without modifying it, and is considered idempotent and safe?
explanation: GET is both safe (does not modify server state) and idempotent (multiple identical requests have the same effect as one). It is used to retrieve resources from the server.

q: Which HTTP method is used to submit data to be processed by the server and is NOT idempotent?
explanation: POST submits data to the server (e.g., form submission). It is not idempotent â€” multiple identical POST requests may create multiple resources.

q: Which HTTP method replaces an entire resource with the provided data and is idempotent?
explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource at the given URI with the supplied data. It is idempotent: sending the same PUT request multiple times yields the same result.

q: Which HTTP method is used to apply partial modifications to a resource?
explanation: PATCH applies a partial update to a resource. Unlike PUT, which replaces the whole resource, PATCH only changes the specified fields.

q: Which HTTP method removes the specified resource from the server?
explanation: DELETE removes the resource identified by the URI. It is idempotent â€” deleting an already-deleted resource still returns a successful or not-found response without side effects.

q: Which HTTP method retrieves only the headers of a response, without the body?
explanation: HEAD is identical to GET but the server returns only the response headers, not the body. It is used to check if a resource exists or to read metadata.

q: Which HTTP method is used to describe the communication options available for the target resource?
explanation: OPTIONS requests the permitted communication options for a given URL. It is widely used in CORS preflight requests to check which methods and headers the server allows.

q: What does HTTP status code 200 indicate?
explanation: HTTP 200 OK means the request was successful. The response body contains the requested resource or result.

q: Which HTTP status code indicates that a new resource has been successfully created?
explanation: 201 Created is returned when a POST or PUT request results in the creation of a new resource. The Location header typically points to the new resource.

q: What does HTTP status code 204 mean?
explanation: 204 No Content means the server successfully processed the request but is not returning any content. Often used for DELETE or PUT responses.

q: Which HTTP status code class indicates informational responses?
explanation: 1xx status codes are informational. For example, 100 Continue tells the client to continue sending the request body.

q: HTTP status code 301 indicates:
explanation: 301 Moved Permanently indicates the resource has been permanently moved to a new URL. Clients and search engines should update their bookmarks.

q: Which status code indicates a temporary redirect where the original URL should still be used for future requests?
explanation: 302 Found (temporary redirect) tells the client the resource is temporarily at a different URI, but future requests should still use the original URL.

q: HTTP status code 304 means:
options: Not Modified â€” use cached version
explanation: 304 Not Modified tells the client that the cached version of the resource is still valid, so no response body is sent. Used with conditional requests (If-Modified-Since / ETag).

q: Which HTTP status code indicates that the client sent a malformed or invalid request?
explanation: 400 Bad Request means the server cannot process the request due to a client error such as malformed syntax or invalid request parameters.

q: What does HTTP status code 401 indicate?
explanation: 401 Unauthorized means authentication is required but has not been provided or is invalid. The client should authenticate and retry.

q: What is the difference between HTTP 401 and HTTP 403?
options: 401 means server error; 403 means client error
401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not authorized
401 means resource not found; 403 means bad request
explanation: 401 Unauthorized means the client needs to authenticate. 403 Forbidden means the client is authenticated but lacks permission to access the resource.

q: HTTP status code 404 means:
options: Not Found â€” resource does not exist
explanation: 404 Not Found means the server could not find the requested resource. It may have been deleted or the URL may be incorrect.

q: Which status code indicates that the HTTP method used is not supported for the resource?
explanation: 405 Method Not Allowed means the request method (e.g., DELETE) is known but not supported for the target resource. The Allow header lists permitted methods.

q: HTTP status code 429 indicates:
options: Too Many Requests â€” rate limit exceeded
explanation: 429 Too Many Requests is returned when the client has sent too many requests in a given time period (rate limiting). The Retry-After header may indicate when to retry.

q: Which HTTP status code indicates an internal server error?
explanation: 500 Internal Server Error is a generic error message indicating an unexpected condition on the server that prevented it from fulfilling the request.

q: HTTP status code 503 means:
explanation: 503 Service Unavailable means the server is temporarily unable to handle the request, usually due to maintenance or overloading. Retry-After header may be included.

q: Which HTTP header specifies the media type of the request or response body?
explanation: Content-Type specifies the MIME type of the request or response body (e.g., application/json, text/html). It tells the receiver how to parse the body.

q: Which HTTP request header tells the server what media types the client can accept?
explanation: The Accept header in a request tells the server which content types (MIME types) the client can process. The server uses this for content negotiation.

q: Which HTTP header is used to send credentials (e.g., Bearer token) to authenticate a request?
explanation: The Authorization header carries credentials to authenticate a client with a server, commonly in the form 'Bearer <token>' for JWT-based auth or 'Basic <base64>' for Basic auth.

q: Which HTTP header controls caching behavior for both requests and responses?
explanation: Cache-Control is the primary HTTP/1.1 header for caching directives. It supports values like no-cache, no-store, max-age, public, and private to control how responses are cached.

q: What is the main difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTP uses port 443; HTTPS uses port 80
HTTPS encrypts data using TLS/SSL; HTTP sends data in plain text
HTTP is faster; HTTPS is slower but more powerful
HTTPS is only for downloading files
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses TLS (formerly SSL) to encrypt data between the client and server, providing confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. HTTP sends data as plain text.

q: What is the default port for HTTPS?
explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 by default. HTTP uses port 80. These are well-known port assignments defined by IANA.

q: Which HTTP version introduced multiplexing, allowing multiple requests over a single TCP connection?
explanation: HTTP/2 introduced multiplexing, which allows multiple concurrent requests and responses over a single TCP connection, eliminating the head-of-line blocking issue of HTTP/1.1.

q: HTTP/1.1 improved over HTTP/1.0 by introducing:
explanation: HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections (keep-alive) by default, so the TCP connection is reused for multiple requests, reducing latency compared to HTTP/1.0.

q: Which transport protocol does HTTP/3 use instead of TCP?
explanation: HTTP/3 uses QUIC, which runs over UDP. QUIC provides built-in encryption, connection migration, and eliminates TCP's head-of-line blocking at the transport layer.

q: Which HTTP/2 feature allows the server to proactively send resources to the client before they are requested?
explanation: HTTP/2 Server Push allows the server to send additional resources (e.g., CSS, JS) to the client without waiting for explicit requests, reducing page load times.

q: What compression algorithm does HTTP/2 use for header compression?
explanation: HTTP/2 uses HPACK for header compression, which reduces overhead by encoding headers efficiently and maintaining a shared compression context between client and server.

q: Which storage mechanism stores data with an expiry date and is sent with every HTTP request to the server?
explanation: Cookies are stored in the browser and automatically sent with every HTTP request to the matching domain. They can have an expiration date and are commonly used for sessions and tracking.

q: Which storage mechanism persists data even after the browser is closed and has no automatic expiry?
explanation: localStorage persists data with no expiration until explicitly cleared by JavaScript or the user. It survives browser restarts and is scoped to the origin (domain + protocol + port).

q: Which storage mechanism clears data when the browser tab or window is closed?
explanation: sessionStorage data is scoped to the browser tab/window. When the tab is closed, all sessionStorage data is cleared. Each tab has its own independent sessionStorage.

q: What is the typical storage limit for localStorage and sessionStorage?
explanation: Most browsers allow 5â€“10 MB of storage for localStorage and sessionStorage per origin. In contrast, cookies are limited to about 4 KB per cookie.

q: What is CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)?
options: A method to compress HTTP responses
A browser security mechanism that controls cross-origin HTTP requests
A protocol for encrypting API calls
A way to cache resources across origins
explanation: CORS is a browser security mechanism implemented via HTTP headers that allows or restricts web pages from making requests to a different origin (domain, protocol, or port) than the one that served the page.

q: Which HTTP response header is required for a server to allow cross-origin requests?
explanation: Access-Control-Allow-Origin is the key CORS response header. It specifies which origins are permitted to access the resource (e.g., '*' for all, or a specific origin).

q: What is a CORS preflight request?
options: A GET request to check server availability
An OPTIONS request sent before a cross-origin request to check server permissions
A POST request with an empty body
A HEAD request to validate CORS headers
explanation: A preflight request is an HTTP OPTIONS request automatically sent by the browser before complex cross-origin requests (e.g., using custom headers or non-simple methods). The server responds with allowed methods, headers, and origins.

q: Which of the following is NOT a REST architectural constraint?
explanation: REST constraints include: Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, and Code on Demand (optional). Persistent Connection is an HTTP/1.1 feature, not a REST constraint.

q: What does 'stateless' mean in the context of REST?
options: The server stores all client session data
Each request from the client must contain all information needed to process it
The client caches all server responses
explanation: REST statelessness means each HTTP request must contain all the information (authentication, context, data) the server needs to process it. The server does not store client state between requests.

q: In RESTful API design, which HTTP method should be used to retrieve a collection of resources?
explanation: GET /users retrieves the collection of user resources. RESTful APIs use GET for read operations, POST for creation, PUT/PATCH for updates, and DELETE for deletion.

q: In RESTful API design, what is the correct endpoint to update a specific resource with id=5?
explanation: PUT /users/5 follows REST conventions: the resource type is in the URL path, the ID identifies the specific resource, and PUT is used for full updates. PATCH /users/5 would be used for partial updates.

q: Which principle of REST ensures that each resource is accessible via a consistent, standardized interface?
explanation: The Uniform Interface constraint is central to REST. It decouples the client from the server implementation and requires resource identification in requests, manipulation through representations, self-descriptive messages, and HATEOAS.

q: What does HATEOAS stand for in REST?
options: Hypertext As The Engine Of Application State
HTTP API Transfer Engine Of Application Service
Hyperlink And Transfer Engine Of API State
High Availability Transfer Engine Of Application Services
explanation: HATEOAS (Hypertext As The Engine Of Application State) is a REST constraint where the server includes hyperlinks in responses, guiding the client on available actions dynamically without prior knowledge.

q: What is the key advantage of stateless communication in RESTful APIs?
options: It allows the server to remember client preferences
It improves scalability as any server can handle any request independently
It reduces the amount of data sent per request
explanation: Statelessness improves scalability: since the server holds no session state, any server in a cluster can handle any request. Load balancing becomes simpler and servers can be scaled horizontally.

q: Which HTTP status code should a RESTful API return when a resource is successfully created via POST?
explanation: 201 Created is the correct response to a successful POST that creates a new resource. The response should include a Location header pointing to the new resource's URL.

q: Which HTTP/2 feature reduces the overhead of repeated headers across multiple requests?
explanation: HPACK is HTTP/2's header compression algorithm. It uses a shared dynamic table to avoid re-sending repeated headers, significantly reducing overhead in applications with many requests.

q: What is the 'Same-Origin Policy' in web browsers?
options: A policy that allows any website to access resources from any other website
A security restriction preventing scripts from accessing resources from a different origin
A caching policy where same resources are stored once
A policy restricting use of cookies to secure origins only
explanation: The Same-Origin Policy prevents scripts on a web page from accessing data from a different origin (protocol + domain + port). CORS is the mechanism to relax this restriction in a controlled way.

q: Which cookie attribute ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS connections?
explanation: The Secure attribute on a cookie ensures it is only transmitted over HTTPS, preventing it from being sent over unencrypted HTTP connections, protecting it from man-in-the-middle attacks.

q: Which cookie attribute prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie, mitigating XSS attacks?
explanation: The HttpOnly flag prevents JavaScript (document.cookie) from accessing the cookie. This mitigates Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks that attempt to steal session cookies.

q: Which of the following is the correct way to start a PHP block in an HTML file?
explanation: PHP code is embedded in HTML using the opening tag <?php and closing tag ?>. This is the standard and most widely supported syntax.

q: In PHP, which superglobal variable is used to collect form data sent via the POST method?
explanation: $_POST is a PHP superglobal that collects form data submitted using the HTTP POST method. $_GET collects data from URL query strings.

q: Which function in PHP is used to connect to a MySQL database using MySQLi extension?
explanation: mysqli_connect() is used to open a new connection to the MySQL server using the MySQLi (MySQL Improved) extension. The older mysql_connect() is deprecated.

q: What does the 'echo' statement do in PHP?
explanation: The echo statement in PHP is used to output one or more strings to the browser. It is a language construct, not a function, so parentheses are optional.

q: In Node.js, which module is used to create an HTTP server?
explanation: The built-in 'http' module in Node.js is used to create HTTP servers. You use require('http') to import it and then http.createServer() to create a server.

q: Which command is used to initialize a new Node.js project and create a package.json file?
explanation: npm init is the command used to initialize a new Node.js project. It prompts you for project details and creates a package.json file to manage dependencies and scripts.

q: In Express.js, which method is used to define a route that responds to HTTP GET requests?
explanation: app.get() in Express.js is used to define a route handler for HTTP GET requests. It takes a path and a callback function with request and response objects.

q: What is the purpose of 'middleware' in Express.js?
options: Functions that execute during the request-response cycle
explanation: Middleware in Express.js are functions that have access to the request object, response object, and the next middleware function. They execute code, modify req/res, end the cycle, or call next().

q: In Express.js, what does the 'next()' function do in a middleware?
options: Passes control to the next middleware function
explanation: The next() function in Express.js middleware passes control to the next middleware function in the stack. If not called, the request will be left hanging.

q: Which Python framework follows the 'batteries included' philosophy with a built-in ORM, admin panel, and authentication?
explanation: Django follows the 'batteries included' philosophy, providing built-in features like ORM, admin interface, authentication, URL routing, and form handling out of the box.

q: In Django, what is the role of 'views.py'?
options: Contains the business logic and returns HTTP responses
explanation: In Django, views.py contains view functions or class-based views that handle web requests and return web responses. They contain the business logic of the application.

q: Which command is used to create a new Django project?
explanation: django-admin startproject <projectname> is the command used to create a new Django project. It creates the project directory structure with settings.py, urls.py, and other files.

q: In Flask, which decorator is used to define a URL route?
explanation: @app.route() is the Flask decorator used to bind a URL to a function. For example, @app.route('/home') maps the /home URL to the decorated function.

q: What is the key difference between Django and Flask?
options: Django uses Python, Flask uses JavaScript
Flask is a micro-framework while Django is a full-stack framework
Flask cannot be deployed on servers
explanation: Flask is a micro-framework that provides minimal functionality and gives developers flexibility to add components as needed. Django is a full-stack framework with many built-in features.

q: In Java web development, what is a Servlet?
options: A Java class that handles HTTP requests and generates responses
explanation: A Java Servlet is a server-side Java class that handles HTTP requests and generates HTTP responses. It runs inside a servlet container like Apache Tomcat.

q: Which method in a Java Servlet is called to handle HTTP GET requests?
explanation: The doGet() method in a Java Servlet is overridden to handle HTTP GET requests. Similarly, doPost() handles POST requests. Both receive HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse parameters.

q: What does JSP stand for in Java web development?
explanation: JSP stands for Java Server Pages. It is a server-side technology that allows embedding Java code directly into HTML pages using special tags, which are then compiled into servlets.

q: In JSP, which tag is used to embed Java code (scriptlet)?
explanation: <% ... %> is the JSP scriptlet tag used to embed Java code in an HTML page. The code inside is executed on the server. <%= %> is used to output a Java expression.

q: What is CGI (Common Gateway Interface)?
options: A standard protocol for web servers to execute programs that generate web content
A type of web server software
A database query language for web applications
explanation: CGI (Common Gateway Interface) is a standard protocol that defines how web server software can delegate the generation of web content to executable files (scripts/programs), enabling dynamic content.

q: What is a major performance drawback of traditional CGI?
options: A new process is created for every HTTP request
explanation: The major drawback of traditional CGI is that a new process is spawned for every HTTP request, which is resource-intensive and slow. Technologies like FastCGI and mod_perl were developed to address this.

q: Which web server is known as the most widely used open-source HTTP server software?
explanation: Apache HTTP Server (often called Apache) is the most widely used open-source HTTP server software in the world. It has been the most popular web server on the internet for decades.

q: Nginx is primarily known for its:
options: High performance, event-driven architecture and reverse proxy capabilities
explanation: Nginx is known for its high performance, stability, rich feature set, simple configuration, and low resource consumption. It uses an event-driven, asynchronous architecture and is widely used as a reverse proxy and load balancer.

q: IIS (Internet Information Services) is a web server developed by:
explanation: IIS (Internet Information Services) is a flexible, secure, and manageable web server developed by Microsoft. It runs on Windows Server and is tightly integrated with the Windows ecosystem.

q: What is 'Server-Side Rendering' (SSR)?
options: Rendering HTML in the browser using JavaScript
Generating the full HTML page on the server before sending it to the client
Compressing CSS files on the server
explanation: Server-Side Rendering (SSR) is where the HTML is fully generated on the server for each request and sent to the client. The browser displays the HTML directly, resulting in faster initial page load.

q: What is 'Client-Side Rendering' (CSR)?
options: The server sends pre-built HTML to the browser
The browser downloads a minimal HTML page and uses JavaScript to render content dynamically
Rendering images on the server before sending
Caching server responses in the browser
explanation: In Client-Side Rendering (CSR), the server sends a minimal HTML file and JavaScript to the browser. The JavaScript then fetches data and builds the HTML in the browser, resulting in a richer interactive experience but potentially slower initial load.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of Server-Side Rendering (SSR) over Client-Side Rendering (CSR)?
options: Better SEO as search engine crawlers see fully rendered HTML
explanation: SSR is better for SEO because search engine crawlers receive fully rendered HTML content rather than an empty HTML shell that requires JavaScript execution to populate content.

q: Which of the following is a popular JavaScript template engine used with Node.js/Express.js?
explanation: EJS (Embedded JavaScript) is a popular template engine for Node.js that lets you generate HTML markup with plain JavaScript. Other popular ones include Pug (Jade) and Handlebars.

q: Jinja2 is a template engine primarily used with which Python framework?
options: Both Flask and Django (though Django has its own DTL)
explanation: Jinja2 is the default template engine for Flask and is also used with other Python frameworks. Django uses its own Django Template Language (DTL), though it can be configured to use Jinja2.

q: In the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture, what does the 'Model' represent?
options: The user interface and presentation layer
The application's data, business logic, and database interactions
The URL routing and request handling
The CSS styling and visual layout
explanation: In MVC, the Model represents the application's data layer. It manages data, business logic, and rules of the application, including database interactions and data validation.

q: In MVC architecture, what is the role of the 'Controller'?
options: Stores and retrieves data from the database
Renders the HTML template for the browser
Acts as an intermediary between Model and View, handling user input
Manages CSS styles and JavaScript files
explanation: In MVC, the Controller handles user input (HTTP requests), invokes appropriate Model operations, and selects the View to render. It acts as the traffic controller between Model and View.

q: Which Django file is analogous to the 'Controller' in MVC architecture?
explanation: In Django's MTV (Model-Template-View) pattern, views.py acts as the controller. Django calls it 'View' but it performs the controller role, handling requests, interacting with models, and selecting templates.

q: What is the purpose of Apache's '.htaccess' file?
options: Configure directory-level settings without changing the main server config
Define CSS styles for Apache default pages
explanation: .htaccess is an Apache configuration file that allows directory-level configuration changes without modifying the main server configuration file. It's used for URL rewriting, access control, redirects, and more.

q: In Nginx configuration, what is a 'server block'?
options: A block that blocks malicious requests
A configuration block that defines settings for a virtual host
A JavaScript block embedded in Nginx config
explanation: In Nginx, a server block (similar to Apache's virtual host) defines configuration settings for a specific virtual server, including the domain name, root directory, SSL settings, and location blocks.

q: What does PHP stand for?
explanation: PHP originally stood for 'Personal Home Page' but now recursively stands for 'PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor'. It is a widely-used open-source server-side scripting language designed for web development.

q: In Node.js, what is the event loop responsible for?
options: Handling asynchronous callbacks and I/O operations without blocking
explanation: The event loop in Node.js is what allows it to perform non-blocking I/O operations. It continuously checks for pending callbacks, I/O events, and timers, enabling asynchronous execution in a single-threaded environment.

q: Which of the following is used to install Express.js in a Node.js project?
explanation: npm install express is the command used to install the Express.js framework in a Node.js project. npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager for Node.js.

q: What is the Django ORM?
options: A JavaScript framework bundled with Django
An Object-Relational Mapper that lets you interact with databases using Python objects
explanation: Django ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) allows developers to interact with databases using Python objects instead of writing raw SQL queries. Models defined in models.py are mapped to database tables.

q: In Flask, what does 'render_template()' function do?
options: Renders an HTML template file and returns it as a string response
Creates a new Flask application instance
explanation: render_template() in Flask is used to render an HTML template file from the templates folder. It processes the template with Jinja2 and returns the rendered HTML as a response to the client.

q: What is the web.xml file in Java Servlet-based applications?
options: A stylesheet for the web interface
The deployment descriptor that configures servlets, mappings, and other settings
explanation: web.xml is the deployment descriptor for Java web applications. It configures servlet definitions, URL mappings, filters, listeners, security constraints, and other application settings for the servlet container.

q: Which Java interface must a servlet class implement (or extend a class that implements it)?
explanation: All servlets must implement the javax.servlet.Servlet interface. In practice, most HTTP servlets extend javax.servlet.http.HttpServlet, which provides implementations for handling HTTP-specific requests.

q: What is a 'Reverse Proxy' in the context of web servers like Nginx?
options: A server that fetches resources on behalf of the client from other servers
A proxy that blocks reverse-engineered requests
A server that directly serves database content
A firewall configuration for blocking incoming traffic
explanation: A reverse proxy sits in front of web servers and forwards client requests to those servers. Nginx is commonly used as a reverse proxy to distribute load, cache responses, provide SSL termination, and hide backend server details.

q: In the context of web servers, what is 'Virtual Hosting'?
options: Running a web server inside a virtual machine only
Hosting multiple websites on a single physical server using the same IP
A cloud hosting service provided by AWS
A server that only hosts virtual reality content
explanation: Virtual Hosting allows a single web server (with one IP address) to host multiple websites/domains. Apache uses Virtual Hosts and Nginx uses Server Blocks to configure multiple sites on one server.

q: Which template engine uses curly braces {{ }} and {% %} syntax and is commonly used with Django?
explanation: Django Template Language (DTL) uses {{ variable }} for variable output and {% tag %} for template tags like loops and conditionals. Jinja2 uses similar syntax. EJS uses <% %> and Pug uses indentation.

q: What is 'session management' in server-side web development?
options: Maintaining state information about a user across multiple HTTP requests
explanation: Session management is the process of maintaining user state across multiple stateless HTTP requests. The server stores session data (login status, cart items, etc.) identified by a session ID, typically stored in a cookie.

q: In PHP, which function is used to start a session?
explanation: session_start() is the PHP function used to start a new session or resume an existing one. It must be called before any output is sent to the browser. Session data is stored in the $_SESSION superglobal.

q: What is the purpose of 'load balancing' in web server configuration?
options: Balancing the CSS load across stylesheets
Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed
Balancing memory usage between RAM and disk
Managing the balance between static and dynamic content
explanation: Load balancing distributes incoming client requests across multiple backend servers to improve throughput, reduce latency, increase fault tolerance, and ensure high availability of web applications.

q: In Express.js, what does 'app.use()' do?
options: Mounts middleware functions at a specified path
explanation: app.use() in Express.js mounts a middleware function or a router at a specified path. It is executed for every request to that path, regardless of HTTP method. It's used for cross-cutting concerns like logging, parsing, and authentication.

q: Which of the following best describes the 'MTV' pattern used in Django?
options: Model-Template-View: Django's variation of MVC where View acts as Controller
explanation: Django uses the MTV (Model-Template-View) pattern, which is its variation of MVC. The Model handles data, the Template is the presentation layer (equivalent to MVC View), and the View contains business logic (equivalent to MVC Controller).

q: What is 'URL routing' in a web framework?
options: The physical network routing of HTTP packets
