explanation: In Shared Hosting, multiple websites share the CPU, RAM, and disk space of a single physical server. It is the most affordable hosting option but offers fewer resources and less control.

q: What is VPS (Virtual Private Server) hosting?
options: Hosting on a dedicated physical server with no sharing
A physical server shared by many users with no isolation
A virtualized server environment with dedicated allocated resources on a shared physical machine
Hosting spread across multiple geographic data centers
explanation: VPS hosting uses virtualization to create isolated virtual servers on a shared physical machine. Each VPS has dedicated CPU, RAM, and storage allocations, providing more control than shared hosting at a lower cost than dedicated hosting.

q: Which web hosting type provides the most resources, control, and security for a single client?
explanation: Dedicated Hosting provides an entire physical server exclusively to one client. This offers maximum resources, full control over the server environment, and the highest level of security, but is the most expensive option.

q: What is a key advantage of Cloud Hosting over traditional Dedicated Hosting?
options: It is always cheaper than dedicated hosting
It offers easy scalability by adding/removing resources on demand
It provides a single physical server for better performance
It requires no technical knowledge to manage
explanation: The primary advantage of Cloud Hosting is scalability. Resources (CPU, RAM, storage) can be scaled up or down dynamically based on demand. You typically pay only for what you use, unlike dedicated servers with fixed capacity.

q: What does SEO stand for?
explanation: SEO stands for Search Engine Optimization. It refers to the practice of optimizing web pages to rank higher in search engine results pages (SERPs), thereby increasing organic (non-paid) traffic.

q: Which HTML tag is MOST important for on-page SEO as it defines the title shown in search engine results?
explanation: The <title> tag defines the title of an HTML page. It appears in the browser tab and as the clickable headline in search engine results pages (SERPs), making it critically important for SEO and click-through rates.

q: What is the purpose of a 'robots.txt' file in SEO?
options: To block hackers from accessing the website
To define which pages search engine crawlers should or should not crawl
To generate an automatic sitemap for the website
To specify the canonical URL for duplicate pages
explanation: The robots.txt file, placed in the root of a website, instructs search engine crawlers (bots) which pages or sections they are allowed or disallowed from crawling and indexing.

q: What is a 'canonical URL' in SEO?
options: The very first URL ever registered for a website
The preferred URL for a page when multiple URLs with duplicate content exist
A URL that uses HTTPS instead of HTTP
A URL shortened for social media sharing
explanation: A canonical URL, specified via the <link rel='canonical' href='...'> tag, tells search engines which version of a URL is the 'master' or preferred version when duplicate or similar content exists at multiple URLs.

q: What does WCAG stand for in web accessibility?
explanation: WCAG stands for Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. It is a set of internationally recognized guidelines published by the W3C's Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI) to make web content more accessible to people with disabilities.

q: WCAG 2.1 is organized around four core principles, abbreviated as POUR. What does 'P' stand for?
explanation: The four WCAG principles are Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust (POUR). 'Perceivable' means information and UI components must be presentable to users in ways they can perceive.

q: According to WCAG, what is the minimum contrast ratio required for normal text to meet Level AA compliance?
explanation: WCAG 2.1 Level AA requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text against its background. For large text (18pt or 14pt bold), the minimum is 3:1. Level AAA requires 7:1 for normal text.

q: What is the purpose of 'alt' text on an tag in relation to web accessibility?
options: It provides a text alternative for images for screen readers and when images fail to load
It is used to apply CSS styles to an image
explanation: The 'alt' attribute provides a textual description of an image. Screen readers read the alt text aloud for visually impaired users. It also displays when an image fails to load and is important for SEO.

q: In the browser rendering pipeline, what is the correct order of the first steps?
options: Parse HTML â†’ Build CSSOM â†’ Build DOM â†’ Create Render Tree
Parse HTML â†’ Build DOM â†’ Parse CSS â†’ Build CSSOM â†’ Create Render Tree
Build DOM â†’ Parse HTML â†’ Build CSSOM â†’ Create Render Tree
Parse CSS â†’ Build CSSOM â†’ Parse HTML â†’ Build DOM â†’ Create Render Tree
explanation: The browser rendering pipeline starts: (1) Parse HTML â†’ Build DOM, (2) Parse CSS â†’ Build CSSOM, (3) Combine DOM + CSSOM â†’ Create Render Tree, (4) Layout (Reflow), (5) Paint, (6) Compositing.

q: What is the 'Render Tree' in the browser rendering pipeline?
options: A tree structure of only the HTML elements
A tree structure of only the CSS rules
A combined tree of visible DOM nodes with their computed styles
A tree representing the JavaScript execution order
explanation: The Render Tree is constructed by combining the DOM and CSSOM. It contains only the visible nodes (elements with 'display:none' are excluded) along with their computed CSS styles. It is used to calculate layout.

q: Which phase in the browser rendering pipeline calculates the exact position and size of each element on the screen?
explanation: The Layout phase (also called Reflow) calculates the exact position, size (width, height), and geometry of every element in the Render Tree based on the viewport size and CSS rules.

q: What is the 'Critical Rendering Path' (CRP)?
options: The fastest network route to a web server
The sequence of steps a browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into pixels on screen
The path taken by a URL through DNS resolution
The order in which JavaScript files are executed
explanation: The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) refers to the sequence of steps the browser must complete to render the initial view of a page: DOM construction, CSSOM construction, Render Tree, Layout, and Paint. Optimizing CRP improves page load performance.

q: Why is CSS considered 'render-blocking'?
options: CSS files are always larger than HTML files
The browser cannot construct the Render Tree and render content until it has downloaded and processed all CSS
Browsers download CSS files before HTML
explanation: CSS is render-blocking because the browser needs the complete CSSOM (from all CSS) before it can create the Render Tree and paint anything to the screen. Loading CSS in the <head> with <link> is standard practice.

q: What is 'minification' in the context of web performance optimization?
options: Reducing image resolution for mobile devices
Removing unnecessary characters (spaces, comments, newlines) from code files without changing functionality
explanation: Minification removes all unnecessary characters from source code (CSS, JS, HTML) â€” spaces, newlines, comments, long variable names â€” to reduce file size without changing its execution. Minified files have a '.min.js' or '.min.css' extension.

q: What is Gzip compression in web performance?
options: A server-side compression method that reduces the size of text-based resources transferred over HTTP
explanation: Gzip is a server-side compression algorithm that compresses text-based files (HTML, CSS, JS) before sending them over the network. The browser decompresses them upon receipt. It can reduce file sizes by 70-90%, significantly improving load times.

q: Which HTTP response header indicates that a resource has been compressed with Gzip?
explanation: 'Content-Encoding: gzip' is the HTTP response header that tells the browser the response body has been encoded (compressed) using gzip. The browser then automatically decompresses it. 'Accept-Encoding: gzip' is the request header sent by the browser.

q: What is 'lazy loading' in web development?
options: Loading all page resources at once before display
Deferring the loading of non-critical resources (images, scripts) until they are needed or visible
Using a slow connection to test page load times
Caching all resources in the browser permanently
explanation: Lazy loading is a performance optimization technique that defers loading of non-critical resources (especially off-screen images and videos) until the user scrolls near them. This reduces initial page load time and saves bandwidth.

q: How is native lazy loading implemented for images in HTML5?
explanation: HTML5 introduced the native 'loading' attribute for and

q: What does the browser's 'Network' tab in Developer Tools primarily show?
options: The DOM structure of the page
All network requests made by the page, including timing, size, and status codes
CPU and memory usage of the browser
explanation: The Network tab in browser Developer Tools displays all HTTP/HTTPS requests made by the page (HTML, CSS, JS, images, API calls), along with their status codes, response headers, file sizes, and detailed timing information.

q: In browser Developer Tools, which panel allows you to inspect and modify the DOM structure and CSS styles in real-time?
explanation: The Elements panel (called Inspector in Firefox) displays the live DOM tree of the page. You can click on any element to view and edit its HTML and see its computed/applied CSS styles in the right-hand pane, with changes reflected immediately.

q: What is W3C?
options: A private company that owns the internet infrastructure
The World Wide Web Consortium â€” an international standards organization for the Web
explanation: W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) is an international community founded by Tim Berners-Lee in 1994 that develops open standards for the Web (HTML, CSS, XML, etc.) to ensure long-term growth and interoperability.

q: Which organization is responsible for publishing the HTML5 specification?
explanation: HTML5 was developed jointly by W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) and WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group). WHATWG maintains the 'living standard' for HTML, while W3C publishes versioned snapshots.

q: In a URL, the path '/products/electronics/laptops' represents what?
options: The specific resource location on the server
explanation: The path in a URL specifies the specific location or resource on the server. In '/products/electronics/laptops', each segment separated by '/' navigates deeper into the server's directory structure or routing hierarchy.

q: What is a CDN (Content Delivery Network) and its primary role in web performance?
options: A database system for storing web content
A network of geographically distributed servers that deliver content to users from the nearest location
A tool for minifying CSS and JavaScript files
A DNS service for fast domain resolution
explanation: A CDN is a network of servers distributed globally. When a user requests content, it is served from the server geographically closest to them, reducing latency and improving load times. CDNs also reduce load on the origin server.

q: What does 'browser caching' do for web performance?
options: It compresses files before sending them to the browser
It stores copies of resources locally so repeat visits don't require re-downloading them
It preloads resources from the server to the CDN
It removes unused CSS and JS from files
explanation: Browser caching stores copies of web resources (images, CSS, JS) in the user's local cache. On subsequent visits, the browser serves these resources from the cache instead of downloading them again, dramatically reducing load times.

q: What is 'TTFB' (Time To First Byte)?
options: The time taken for a browser to render the first visible pixel
The time from the user's request to when the browser receives the first byte of the server's response
The time for DNS resolution to complete
The time taken to fully load all images on a page
explanation: TTFB (Time To First Byte) measures the time from when a client sends an HTTP request to when it receives the first byte of the server's response. It reflects server processing time and network latency. A good TTFB is under 200ms.

q: What is a 'sitemap.xml' file and its purpose?
options: A file that blocks search engine crawlers from certain pages
An XML file that lists all pages of a website to help search engines discover and index them
A JavaScript file that generates the site's navigation menu
A configuration file for web server routing
explanation: A sitemap.xml is an XML file submitted to search engines that lists all the important URLs of a website. It helps search engine crawlers discover pages they might otherwise miss, especially for large or newly created sites.

q: Which of the following is an 'Operable' principle guideline under WCAG?
options: All images must have alt text
All functionality must be accessible via keyboard
Text must have sufficient color contrast
Web pages must have a consistent navigation structure
explanation: The 'Operable' principle in WCAG requires that UI components and navigation must be operable. A key guideline is that all functionality must be accessible using only a keyboard, without requiring a mouse â€” critical for users with motor disabilities.

q: What is the purpose of the 'Compositing' step in the browser rendering pipeline?
options: To calculate the position and size of elements
To draw pixels onto the screen
To merge multiple layer bitmaps in the correct order to produce the final visible page
To parse and apply CSS styles
explanation: Compositing is the final step in the rendering pipeline. The browser separates page content into layers (especially for animated or transformed elements), paints each layer separately, and then the GPU composites (merges) them in the correct order to produce the final frame.

q: What is 'reflow' (layout thrashing) in browser performance?
options: The browser re-downloading all CSS files
When JavaScript repeatedly reads and writes DOM properties that force the browser to recalculate layout multiple times
The process of re-parsing HTML after a JavaScript change
explanation: Reflow (or layout thrashing) occurs when JavaScript alternately reads and writes DOM layout properties (like offsetWidth), causing the browser to repeatedly recalculate the layout. This is a major performance bottleneck and should be minimized by batching DOM reads/writes.

q: What does the 'defer' attribute do when added to a <script> tag?
options: It prevents the script from ever executing
It downloads the script asynchronously and executes it after the HTML parsing is complete
It downloads and executes the script immediately, pausing HTML parsing
It loads the script from a CDN
explanation: The 'defer' attribute on a <script> tag tells the browser to download the script in the background without blocking HTML parsing, and to execute it only after the DOM is fully parsed. Scripts with defer execute in order, unlike 'async'.

q: What is an 'authoritative name server' in DNS?
options: The first DNS server queried by the browser
The root server that knows all domain names
The server that holds the actual DNS records for a specific domain
A caching server that stores recently queried DNS records
explanation: The Authoritative Name Server is the final authority for a domain's DNS records. It holds the actual A, CNAME, MX, and other DNS records for a domain. When the recursive resolver reaches it, it gets the definitive IP address.

q: What is the difference between 'on-page SEO' and 'off-page SEO'?
options: On-page SEO deals with server configuration; off-page SEO deals with HTML content
On-page SEO refers to optimizations made on the website itself; off-page SEO refers to external factors like backlinks
On-page SEO is for mobile; off-page SEO is for desktop
On-page SEO uses paid advertising; off-page SEO uses organic methods
explanation: On-page SEO involves optimizations within your website (title tags, meta descriptions, content quality, URL structure, internal linking). Off-page SEO refers to external signals, primarily backlinks from other reputable websites, that influence your site's authority and ranking.

q: What is 'ARIA' in the context of web accessibility?
options: Accessible Rich Internet Applications â€” a set of HTML attributes to enhance accessibility for dynamic content
explanation: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) is a specification from W3C's WAI that defines a set of HTML attributes (role, aria-label, aria-hidden, etc.) to make dynamic and complex web applications more accessible to users with assistive technologies like screen readers.

q: Which browser Developer Tools panel would you use to record and analyze CPU usage, JavaScript execution time, and rendering performance?
explanation: The Performance panel (previously called Timeline) in browser Developer Tools allows you to record a profile of the page. It shows a detailed waterfall of JavaScript execution, rendering, painting, and compositing, helping identify bottlenecks.

q: What is the purpose of the 'meta viewport' tag for web performance and responsive design?
options: It defines the character encoding of the page
It controls how the browser scales and displays the page on mobile devices
It specifies keywords for search engine indexing
It sets the page refresh interval
explanation: The meta viewport tag (<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'>) instructs the browser on how to control the page's dimensions and scaling on mobile devices, essential for responsive web design.

q: What is 'tree shaking' in the context of JavaScript bundling and web performance?
options: Removing unused HTML elements from the DOM
A technique to eliminate unused (dead) code from JavaScript bundles at build time
Clearing the browser's cache and cookies
Reorganizing the DOM tree for faster rendering
explanation: Tree shaking is a dead code elimination technique used by modern JavaScript bundlers (like Webpack, Rollup). It analyzes import/export statements and removes code that is never actually used, reducing bundle size and improving load performance.

q: What is a 'subdomain' in the context of URL structure?
options: The path after the domain name
A prefix to the main domain name separated by a dot, e.g., 'blog' in 'blog.example.com'
The TLD portion of a domain
The port number in a URL
explanation: A subdomain is a prefix that appears before the main domain name, separated by a dot. For example, in 'blog.example.com', 'blog' is the subdomain, 'example' is the domain, and '.com' is the TLD. Subdomains can point to different servers or sections of a site.

q: What does 'above the fold' content mean in web performance optimization?
options: Content that is only visible after user authentication
Content visible in the browser viewport without any scrolling
Content loaded from a CDN server
CSS content that appears before JavaScript on the page
explanation: 'Above the fold' refers to the portion of a webpage visible without scrolling. Optimizing above-the-fold content (inline critical CSS, prioritizing these resources) improves perceived performance and user experience by showing useful content faster.
